title: MCV4U Exit Card: Curve Sketching with JSXGraph Figures

drawingProjectChoice.reason: This lesson depends on function curves, derivative sign evidence, asymptotes, and classroom graph discussion. JSXGraph is designed for interactive geometry and function plotting in the browser, while the package keeps local SVG fallbacks for stable review and answer locking.
drawingProjectChoice.currentOutput: review.html uses static SVG fallbacks and can optionally render JSXGraph-backed figures from figureSpec.

notSelectedAsPrimary.reason: Excellent React math visuals, but this artifact is static HTML and needs rational/asymptote classroom review first.
Better for MDM/statistics than calculus curve sketching.
Better for explanatory videos than a 12-minute diagnostic card.

problemAnchor: How do domain, intercepts, asymptotes, f', f'', critical points, and inflection points work together to produce an accurate curve sketch?

abt.and: Students reviewed a step-by-step curve-sketching algorithm and applied it to polynomial and rational examples.
abt.but: Students can mix up intercepts, asymptotes, critical numbers, and inflection points when the graph has to be assembled.
abt.therefore: The exit card checks whether they can use derivative and domain evidence to justify the sketch, not just recognize a final picture.

objective: Students can produce and explain a calculus-based curve sketch using domain, intercepts/asymptotes, first derivative sign, second derivative sign, extrema, and inflection points.

knowledgeNodes.name: Domain and continuity
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher began the algorithm by identifying whether the function is polynomial, rational, root, continuous, or restricted.

knowledgeNodes.name: Intercepts and asymptotes
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: The cubic example has intercepts 0 and 3 and no asymptotes; the rational example has vertical and oblique asymptotes.

knowledgeNodes.name: First derivative test
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher used f'(x)=0 and sign charts to decide increasing/decreasing intervals and local extrema.

knowledgeNodes.name: Second derivative test
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher used f''(x)=0 and sign charts to decide concavity and points of inflection.

knowledgeNodes.name: Integrated curve sketch
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher repeatedly connected domain, intercepts, f', f'', extrema, inflection, and final sketch.

knowledgeNodes.name: Relationship between f, f', and f''
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Near the end, teacher asked students to identify f, f', and f'' from graph behavior.

errorTaxonomy.meaning: Treats all functions the same and misses that polynomial vs rational changes domain/asymptote work.
Does not correctly solve f'(x)=0 or connect critical numbers to local extrema.
Can make or read a sign chart but cannot translate signs into increasing/decreasing intervals.
Uses f''=0 mechanically without checking concavity change or point coordinates.
Misses vertical restrictions or oblique asymptote from long division in rational curve sketching.
Cannot connect f's slope to f' and f's concavity to f''.
Knows isolated facts but cannot assemble a coherent final curve-sketching summary.

answerLock.noHorizontalAsymptoteReason: numerator degree is exactly one more than denominator degree, so use long division for an oblique asymptote

relationshipRules: If f is increasing, f' is above the x-axis.
If f is decreasing, f' is below the x-axis.
If f changes concavity, f'' changes sign.
Points of inflection are located on f, not on f'' itself.

questions.stem: For the lesson example f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x, what should be recorded first in a curve-sketching solution?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a polynomial, so its domain is all real numbers and it is continuous everywhere.
options.feedbackCorrect: Yes. The teacher started by classifying the function type and domain.

options.label: B
options.text: It has a vertical asymptote at x=0 because x=0 is an intercept.
options.feedbackWrong: An intercept is not a discontinuity. Polynomials do not have vertical asymptotes.

options.label: C
options.text: It is undefined at x=3 because f(3)=0.
options.feedbackWrong: f(3)=0 means an x-intercept, not an undefined point.

options.label: D
options.text: The domain can only be found after the second derivative.
options.feedbackWrong: Domain and continuity come before derivative analysis.

questions.stem: When f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x is factored to find intercepts, which x-intercepts appear in the lesson?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. The teacher identified roots 0 and 3.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Those are critical numbers from f'(x)=0, not both intercepts.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: x=2 is the inflection x-value in the cubic example.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: x=1 is the local maximum x-value, not an x-intercept.

questions.stem: For f'(x)=3x^2-12x+9, which critical numbers are used in the first derivative sign chart?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Yes. Solving f'(x)=0 gives x=1 and x=3.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Those are intercepts of f, not the two critical numbers from f'.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: x=2 comes from f''(x)=0, so it is the inflection candidate.

options.label: D
options.text: No critical numbers, because the function is continuous.
options.feedbackWrong: Continuity does not remove critical numbers; use f'(x)=0.

questions.stem: Using the sign chart, where is f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x decreasing?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. f' is negative between 1 and 3.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: f' is positive there, so the function is increasing.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: f' is positive to the right of 3.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: That interval belongs to concavity from f'', not decreasing from f'.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly connects the graph and the first derivative test?

options.label: A
options.text: (1,4) is a local maximum and (3,0) is a local minimum.
options.feedbackCorrect: Yes. f' changes + to - at 1 and - to + at 3.

options.label: B
options.text: (1,4) is an x-intercept because x=1 is critical.
options.feedbackWrong: Critical numbers are not automatically intercepts.

options.label: C
options.text: (3,0) cannot be a local minimum because it is also an x-intercept.
options.feedbackWrong: A point can be both an intercept and a local minimum.

options.label: D
options.text: (2,2) is the local maximum because f''(2)=0.
options.feedbackWrong: f''(2)=0 marks a possible inflection point, not a maximum by itself.

questions.stem: For f''(x)=6x-12, what is the possible inflection x-value, and what point is on the original graph?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. Set f''=0, then substitute x=2 into f.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: x=1 is from f'=0 and gives a local maximum.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: x=3 is a critical number and local minimum.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: x=0 is an intercept, not the f''=0 value.

questions.stem: According to the second derivative sign chart, what happens at x=2?

options.label: A
options.text: The graph changes from concave down to concave up.
options.feedbackCorrect: Yes. f'' changes from negative to positive.

options.label: B
options.text: The graph changes from increasing to decreasing.
options.feedbackWrong: That would be read from f', not f''.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: The cubic is continuous everywhere.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: The point at x=2 is (2,2), not an x-intercept.

questions.stem: In the rational example r(x)=x^2/(x-1), which asymptote information matches the lesson's method?

options.label: A
options.text: Vertical asymptote x=1 and oblique asymptote y=x+1.
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct. Denominator zero gives x=1; long division gives y=x+1.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: An intercept does not remove denominator restrictions.

options.label: C
options.text: Horizontal asymptote y=0 because the function is rational.
options.feedbackWrong: Here the numerator degree is one higher, so use an oblique asymptote.

options.label: D
options.text: Vertical asymptote x=0 because the graph has an x-intercept there.
options.feedbackWrong: Vertical asymptotes come from denominator restrictions, not x-intercepts.

questions.stem: Explain how you would decide which graph is f, f', and f'' if the labels were removed. Use slope and concavity evidence.
questions.rubric: A strong answer states that f' is positive where f increases and negative where f decreases, and that f'' is positive where f is concave up and negative where f is concave down. It should mention that x-axis crossings/sign changes of f' and f'' correspond to extrema and inflection evidence.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would start with the graph I think is f and check its slope. Where f is increasing, f' should be above the x-axis; where f is decreasing, f' should be below. Then I would check concavity: where f bends upward, f'' should be positive, and where it bends downward, f'' should be negative.

questions.stem: Write a curve-sketching summary for f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x. Include domain/continuity, intercepts, local extrema, inflection point, and concavity.
questions.rubric: A strong answer includes all real domain and continuity, intercepts (0,0) and (3,0), local maximum (1,4), local minimum (3,0), inflection point (2,2), increasing/decreasing intervals, and concave down/up intervals.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The function is a polynomial, so it is continuous for all real x and has no asymptotes. Its x-intercepts are (0,0) and (3,0). From f', it increases on (-infinity,1), decreases on (1,3), then increases on (3,infinity), so (1,4) is a local maximum and (3,0) is a local minimum. From f'', it is concave down before x=2 and concave up after x=2, so (2,2) is an inflection point.

diagnosisRules.systemAction: mark passed and continue normal homework or next lesson
diagnosisRules.studentMessage: 你已经抓住了这节课 curve sketching 的核心证据链。

diagnosisRules.systemAction: assign reteach modules matching missed errorIds, then retest
diagnosisRules.studentMessage: 你有几个环节还没有连稳。先做对应补救，再复测确认。
这节课的曲线草图框架还没建立，需要重新走一遍核心流程。

reteachModules.trigger: Treats all functions the same and misses that polynomial vs rational changes domain/asymptote work.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Treats all functions the same and misses that polynomial vs rational changes domain/asymptote work.

miniLesson.explain: Start curve sketching by classifying the function. A polynomial has domain all real numbers and is continuous everywhere; a rational function needs denominator restrictions.
miniLesson.example: For f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x, there is no denominator or root restriction, so no vertical asymptote appears.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Function type first: polynomial means no breaks; rational means check the denominator.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: Start curve sketching by classifying the function.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: For f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x, there is no denominator or root restriction, so no vertical asymptote appears.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: Function type first: polynomial means no breaks; rational means check the denominator.

miniLesson.explain: Intercepts are where the graph crosses an axis. Asymptotes come from restrictions or end behavior. They answer different questions.
miniLesson.example: For the cubic, x=0 and x=3 are intercepts. For r(x)=x^2/(x-1), x=1 is not an intercept; it is a vertical asymptote because the denominator is zero.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Zero of numerator can be an intercept; zero of denominator can be an asymptote.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: Intercepts are where the graph crosses an axis.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: For the cubic, x=0 and x=3 are intercepts. For r(x)=x^2/(x-1), x=1 is not an intercept; it is a vertical asymptote because the denominator is zero.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: Zero of numerator can be an intercept; zero of denominator can be an asymptote.

reteachModules.trigger: Does not correctly solve f'(x)=0 or connect critical numbers to local extrema.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Does not correctly solve f'(x)=0 or connect critical numbers to local extrema.

miniLesson.explain: Critical numbers come from f'(x)=0 or where f' is undefined. They are x-values to test for local extrema.
miniLesson.example: For f'(x)=3x^2-12x+9, factoring gives 3(x-1)(x-3), so x=1 and x=3.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: f' zero gives candidates; the sign change gives the verdict.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: Critical numbers come from f'(x)=0 or where f' is undefined.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: For f'(x)=3x^2-12x+9, factoring gives 3(x-1)(x-3), so x=1 and x=3.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: f' zero gives candidates; the sign change gives the verdict.

reteachModules.trigger: Can make or read a sign chart but cannot translate signs into increasing/decreasing intervals.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Can make or read a sign chart but cannot translate signs into increasing/decreasing intervals.

miniLesson.explain: A positive f' interval means f is increasing; a negative f' interval means f is decreasing. The sign chart must become interval language.
miniLesson.example: The cubic has f'>0 before 1, f'<0 between 1 and 3, and f'>0 after 3.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: f' sign is the direction of travel.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: A positive f' interval means f is increasing; a negative f' interval means f is decreasing.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: The cubic has f'>0 before 1, f'<0 between 1 and 3, and f'>0 after 3.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: f' sign is the direction of travel.

reteachModules.trigger: Uses f''=0 mechanically without checking concavity change or point coordinates.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Uses f''=0 mechanically without checking concavity change or point coordinates.

miniLesson.explain: f'' controls concavity. A point of inflection requires a change in concavity, and the final point must be on f, not on f''.
miniLesson.example: f''(x)=6x-12 gives x=2. Since f'' changes from negative to positive, f(2)=2 gives inflection point (2,2).
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Find x on f'', but report the point on f.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: f'' controls concavity.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: f''(x)=6x-12 gives x=2. Since f'' changes from negative to positive, f(2)=2 gives inflection point (2,2).

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: Find x on f'', but report the point on f.

reteachModules.trigger: Misses vertical restrictions or oblique asymptote from long division in rational curve sketching.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Misses vertical restrictions or oblique asymptote from long division in rational curve sketching.

miniLesson.explain: For rational functions, set the denominator to zero for vertical asymptotes, then compare degrees. If the numerator degree is exactly one higher, use long division for an oblique asymptote.
miniLesson.example: x^2/(x-1) has vertical asymptote x=1 and long division gives y=x+1.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Denominator gives the wall; division gives the slant.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: For rational functions, set the denominator to zero for vertical asymptotes, then compare degrees.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: x^2/(x-1) has vertical asymptote x=1 and long division gives y=x+1.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: Denominator gives the wall; division gives the slant.

reteachModules.trigger: Cannot connect f's slope to f' and f's concavity to f''.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Cannot connect f's slope to f' and f's concavity to f''.

miniLesson.explain: Use graph behavior as evidence. f' is tied to slope of f; f'' is tied to concavity of f.
miniLesson.example: If f is increasing over an interval, the f' graph should be above the x-axis over that interval.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: f' reads slope; f'' reads bend.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: Use graph behavior as evidence.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: If f is increasing over an interval, the f' graph should be above the x-axis over that interval.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: f' reads slope; f'' reads bend.

reteachModules.trigger: Knows isolated facts but cannot assemble a coherent final curve-sketching summary.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Knows isolated facts but cannot assemble a coherent final curve-sketching summary.

miniLesson.explain: A complete curve sketch is a chain: domain, intercepts/asymptotes, f' sign, f'' sign, then final graph behavior.
miniLesson.example: The cubic summary needs continuity, intercepts, local max/min, inflection point, increasing/decreasing, and concavity.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: List the evidence before drawing the curve.

guidedPractice.prompt: Name the part of the curve-sketching algorithm this error belongs to.
guidedPractice.answer: A complete curve sketch is a chain: domain, intercepts/asymptotes, f' sign, f'' sign, then final graph behavior.

guidedPractice.prompt: Apply the rule to the cubic f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x or the rational r(x)=x^2/(x-1).
guidedPractice.answer: The cubic summary needs continuity, intercepts, local max/min, inflection point, increasing/decreasing, and concavity.

guidedPractice.prompt: Write one sentence connecting the evidence to the final graph behavior.
guidedPractice.answer: List the evidence before drawing the curve.

questions.stem: Which function type guarantees continuity for all real x?
options: The option supported by derivative/domain evidence
The option based only on the picture
The option that confuses intercept and asymptote
The option that skips the sign chart

questions.stem: Explain why a polynomial curve sketch begins differently from a rational curve sketch.
questions.rubric: Answer must name the correct calculus evidence, apply it to the function or interval, and state the resulting graph behavior.

retestCards.passRule: 2/2 correct, or teacher approves the open response as conceptually sound.

The option based only on the picture

teacherEscalation.condition: student fails the same retest twice or scores 0-4 on two consecutive MCV diagnostics
teacherEscalation.message: 该学生需要人工短辅导；问题已经从单题错误变成 curve sketching 证据链没有建立。
